title: 7 Best Facebook Marketplace Alternatives in 2025 (Ranked)
metaDescription: Tired of Facebook Marketplace? We ranked the 7 best alternatives in 2025—including one that gives you $40 just for signing up.
heroImageAlt: A person comparing multiple online marketplace apps on a laptop screen with items for sale spread on a table beside them
content:
7 Best Facebook Marketplace Alternatives in 2025 (Ranked)

Facebook Marketplace used to feel like a secret weapon for buying and selling locally. Then came the scammers, the "is this still available?" ghosts, and the uncomfortable reality that every transaction is tied to your personal Facebook profile. If you've had enough, you're not alone—and you've got options. Good ones. We tested and ranked the 7 best Facebook Marketplace alternatives in 2025, and the winner might surprise you.

Quick Comparison: Facebook Marketplace vs. The Best Alternatives

Platform Fees Currency Local Pickup Ships Privacy Verdict
Swapedly No fees Swap Bucks (1 SB = $1) Yes Yes No social login required 🏆 Best Overall
OfferUp 12.9% min $1.99 Cash / card Yes Yes Account required Good for locals
Craigslist Free (most) Cash Yes Rarely Email anonymization Bare-bones but free
Mercari No fees Cash / card Yes Yes Account required Great for shipping
Nextdoor Free Cash Yes No Neighborhood-verified Hyperlocal only
Poshmark $2.95 flat / 20% Cash / card No Yes Account required Fashion-focused
eBay 13.25% Cash / card No Yes Account required Global reach, high fees

#1 Swapedly — The Best Facebook Marketplace Alternative in 2025

Let's start with the elephant in the room: Swapedly is genuinely different, and that's actually a good thing. Instead of dealing in cash (which requires awkward meetups) or PayPal (which comes with its own drama), Swapedly runs on Swap Bucks (SB) — a platform currency where 1 SB equals exactly $1 in value. You sell something, earn SB, and spend them on anything else listed on the platform. It's frictionless in a way that cash never quite is.

The fee structure is refreshingly honest: no transaction fees. So if you sell a $500 gaming PC, you pay $50. But sell a $500 bike? You pay $20 max. Compare that to eBay's 13.25% (no cap) on a $500 item — that's $66.25 gone. The math adds up fast.

New users get a $40 Swap Bucks gift card just for signing up — that's 40 SB you can spend immediately on the platform. No hoops, no referral chain required. You can grab that at swapedly.com.

One thing that really stands out: no Facebook account required. This sounds minor until you realize how much data Facebook harvests from Marketplace activity, or until you've had a stranger find your real name and profile after a transaction. Swapedly keeps things clean and separate.

Sellers can offer local pickup or shipping — whichever works for them. Want to upgrade to Swapedly Plus for $9.99/month? You'll earn 1.5x SB on every sale and get 5 featured listings per month. But there's no pressure — the platform works perfectly fine on the free tier.

Swapedly Pros

  • Swapedly charges no transaction fees. The app is clean, the local feed is genuinely useful, and most transactions still happen in person with cash. That's great if you prefer old-school exchanges — not so great if you're trying to avoid the "can we meet in a parking lot at 9pm?" energy.

    Fees for shipped items run 12.9% with a $1.99 minimum, which stings on lower-priced items. Sell a $10 book and you're paying nearly 20% effective fee. Their TruYou verification adds some safety, but it's optional, so you're still rolling the dice on strangers.

    OfferUp Pros

    • Large local user base in most US cities
    • Free to list, cash transactions for local deals
    • Clean, well-designed mobile app

    OfferUp Cons

    • 12.9% shipping fee is higher than competitors
    • No privacy-first alternative to social login
    • Cash-only local deals mean no buyer/seller protection

    #3 Craigslist — Best Free Option (If You're Patient)

    Craigslist is the oldest trick in the book, and somehow it's still standing in 2025. For big items — furniture, appliances, cars — it's genuinely hard to beat because it's completely free. No fees, no shipping logistics, no platform taking a cut. You post, someone calls (or texts, mercifully), you meet, done.

    But the interface looks like it was designed in 2003, because it was. There's no buyer protection, no built-in messaging safety, and scam listings are a real problem. It's essentially the digital equivalent of a classified ad in a newspaper — powerful in concept, rough in practice.

    Craigslist Pros

    • Completely free for most categories
    • Massive reach in most US cities
    • No account required to browse

    Craigslist Cons

    • No buyer or seller protection whatsoever
    • Dated interface, poor mobile experience
    • Scam listings are common

    #4 Mercari — Best for Shipping Small Items

    Mercari hits a sweet spot for people who want to ship items without the complexity of eBay. The app is approachable, listing is quick, and their no transaction fee is straightforward. They've also leaned hard into prepaid shipping labels, which removes one major headache from the process.

    The catch? Buyers pay a separate service fee (around 3-4%), which can sour deals when buyers realize the final price is higher than listed. And Mercari's customer support has a reputation for being slow to resolve disputes.

    Mercari Pros

    • Simple no transaction fee
    • Good shipping integration with prepaid labels
    • Easy, fast listing process

    Mercari Cons

    • Buyers pay additional service fees
    • Customer support can be slow
    • No fee cap — 10% on a $500 item is $50

    #5 Nextdoor — Best for True Hyperlocal Selling

    Nextdoor is less a marketplace and more a neighborhood bulletin board that happens to have a For Sale section. The verification system is its biggest feature — everyone on the platform is verified to their neighborhood, which dramatically cuts down on scam accounts.

    The tradeoff is severe limitations: no shipping, no payment processing, purely local and cash-based. If you're selling a couch to someone three blocks away, perfect. If you want any kind of reach beyond your immediate neighborhood, look elsewhere.

    Nextdoor Pros

    • Neighborhood-verified buyers — very safe
    • Free to list, no fees
    • Great community vibe for local transactions

    Nextdoor Cons

    • No shipping — local pickup only
    • Very limited reach outside your neighborhood
    • No payment processing or buyer protection

    #6 Poshmark — Best for Fashion Sellers

    Poshmark carved out a strong niche in the secondhand fashion world, and for clothing, shoes, and accessories, it's still a top choice. The community aspect — sharing listings, following sellers — creates genuine engagement that most marketplaces can't replicate.

    The fees, though, are brutal: $2.95 flat on anything under $15, and 20% on everything above. That's the highest rate on this list by a significant margin. A $100 dress nets you $80. A $50 jacket nets you $40. It adds up.

    Poshmark Pros

    • Strong fashion-focused community
    • Built-in social features drive discovery
    • Reliable shipping through Poshmark's label system

    Poshmark Cons

    • 20% fee is extremely high
    • Fashion-only focus limits use cases
    • No local pickup option

    #7 eBay — Best for Rare or Collectible Items

    eBay's global reach is unmatched, and for rare items — vintage electronics, collectibles, specialty gear — it's often the only place with enough buyers to get a fair price. The auction format can also work in your favor if demand is high.

    But eBay's 13.25% final value fee is the highest effective rate on this list, and that's before payment processing fees. The platform is also complex enough that casual sellers often make costly mistakes. It's powerful, but overkill for most everyday secondhand selling.

    eBay Pros

    • Largest buyer pool of any marketplace
    • Auction format great for rare items
    • Strong buyer and seller protection

    eBay Cons

    • 13.25% fee — highest on this list
    • Complex fee structure with many add-ons
    • Steep learning curve for new sellers

    So Which Facebook Marketplace Alternative Should You Use?

    It really comes down to what matters most to you. If you want privacy, a fair fee structure, and the freedom to buy and sell without a Facebook account, Swapedly is the clear winner. The Swapedly charges no transaction fees.

    If you're selling a couch locally and just want cash in hand, Craigslist or Nextdoor will do the job for free. But for anything with real value — electronics, sporting goods, furniture — Swapedly's structure rewards you more.

    And yes, the $40 gift card for new users is a legitimate, no-strings perk. That's 40 SB to spend right away. Not bad for five minutes of signup.

    Want to see how Swapedly compares in other categories? Check out our guide to the best places to sell stuff online in 2025 or our breakdown of the best eBay alternatives if fees are your main concern.
